summary:
One of the most striking features of [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]]'s symbolic world was the prominent role of the bull — specifically the aurochs (Bos primigenius), the massive wild ancestor of domestic cattle — in the settlement's art and ritual practice. Bucrania (skulls and horn cores of aurochs and cattle) were frequently mounted on the interior walls of houses, plastered into benches, and incorporated into elaborate plaster installations, creating striking visual features within domestic spaces that also served as burial sites. Wall paintings depicting hunting scenes with aurochs and reliefs of bovine heads further emphasize the centrality of the bull in the community's symbolic repertoire.

The prevalence of bull imagery at Çatalhöyük is particularly striking because the aurochs appears to have been a wild animal hunted rather than a domesticated one managed as livestock during at least the early phases of the settlement. This suggests that the bull's ritual significance was connected to its qualities as a powerful wild creature — strength, unpredictability, and the danger of the hunt — rather than to its role as a domestic animal. The bull may have served as a symbol of male power, dangerous wildness, or the intersection between the human and animal worlds.

The bull cult at Çatalhöyük anticipates the importance of bovine imagery in later Near Eastern and Mediterranean religious traditions, including the bull cult of the Minoan civilization, the sacred bull Apis of Egypt, and the bull-related symbolism of the Mesopotamian god Enlil. Whether these later traditions represent cultural continuity with the Çatalhöyük tradition or independent parallel developments remains an open question, but the antiquity of bovine religious symbolism demonstrated at Çatalhöyük is beyond doubt.
